lessons.title: Reading Between the Lines
lessons.passageText:
Maria woke up early and looked out her window. The sky was gray and heavy with clouds. She could hear the wind blowing through the trees outside. Her mother was already in the kitchen, and the smell of hot chocolate drifted up the stairs. Maria smiled and reached for her thickest sweater.

At school, their teacher Mrs. Santos canceled their outdoor reading session. Instead, she brought out a box of board games and told the class to push their desks together. Everyone cheered. By lunchtime, the first drops began to fall against the classroom windows, making a soft tapping sound.

On the way home, Maria and her classmate Jun shared an umbrella. Their shoes got wet anyway, splashing through the puddles along the path. When Maria finally got home, her lola had already prepared a big pot of arroz caldo.

"I knew you'd need something warm," her lola said with a smile.

Maria changed into dry clothes and sat at the kitchen table. Outside, the rain fell steadily. She wrapped both hands around her bowl and took a slow sip. It tasted like comfort.

questions.questionText: What is the weather most likely like in the story?
How do you know it is cold without the story saying so directly?

choices.choiceText: Maria wore a thick sweater and drank hot chocolate

questions.questionText: Why did Mrs. Santos cancel the outdoor reading session?
What does the arroz caldo tell you about Maria's lola?

choices.choiceText: She likes to cook new recipes
She did not know it was raining

questions.questionText: The last line says the arroz caldo 'tasted like comfort.' What does this mean?

choices.choiceText: It was a new flavor Maria had never tried
It made Maria feel warm and safe

questions.questionText: What can you infer about Jun from the story?

choices.choiceText: Jun is a classmate who walks the same way home
Jun forgot to bring an umbrella
Jun does not like the rain

questions.questionText: Maria reached for her thickest ________ when she saw the gray sky.
Mrs. Santos brought out ________ for the class to play indoors.
Maria and Jun shared an ________ on the way home.
Maria's lola prepared a big pot of ________ for her when she got home.
The rain made a soft ________ sound against the classroom windows.
Reading clues in a story to figure out something that is not directly stated is called ________.

lessons.title: Words That Describe and Do
lessons.passageText:
Every Saturday morning, Lando and his older sister Ate Bea walk to the wet market near their house. The market is loud and crowded, filled with the smell of fresh fish and ripe mangoes. Vendors shout out their prices, and shoppers squeeze past each other carrying heavy baskets.

Ate Bea writes a long list before they leave. She moves quickly through the stalls, picking up smooth purple eggplants, crisp green beans, and a bunch of small sweet bananas. Lando helps by carrying the lighter bags.

At the fish section, a vendor holds up a shiny silver milkfish. "Fresh bangus!" he calls out in a loud, cheerful voice. Ate Bea examines it carefully, pressing the flesh gently.

"Perfect," she says, and nods.

They pay and move on. By the time they finish, their bags are bulging and heavy. Lando's arms ache, but he feels proud.

"Good job today," Ate Bea says, ruffling his hair.

Lando grins. Even though his arms are tired, he always looks forward to their Saturday market trips.

questions.questionText: Which word in the passage is an adjective that describes the eggplants?
Which of the following is a verb from the passage?
In the sentence 'The market is loud and crowded,' what are 'loud' and 'crowded'?
Which sentence uses a verb correctly?
What does the adjective 'crisp' tell you about the green beans?
Adjectives are words that describe ________.
Ate Bea moves ________ through the stalls at the market.
The vendor holds up a ________ silver milkfish.
A word that describes a noun is called an ________.
A word that shows an action or state of being is called a ________.
Ate Bea ________ the fish carefully by pressing the flesh.
Even though his arms are tired, Lando always ________ forward to their Saturday trips.

lessons.title: Step by Step
lessons.passageText:
Every Sunday, Nanay makes her famous puto for the whole family. It is a tradition that has been passed down from her own mother, and the whole house smells wonderful when she is done.

First, Nanay measures out two cups of rice flour and mixes it with sugar and a pinch of salt in a large bowl. Then she slowly pours in coconut milk, stirring all the while to make sure there are no lumps. Next, she adds baking powder and mixes again until the batter is smooth and a little thick.

After mixing, she greases the small puto molds with a little butter so the puto will slide out easily when cooked. She fills each mold about three-quarters full, because the puto will rise as it steams. On top of each one, she places a small square of cheese or a slice of salted egg.

Finally, Nanay arranges the filled molds inside the steamer. She covers the pot and lets the puto steam for about fifteen to twenty minutes. When the tops spring back lightly when touched, they are done.

She lets them cool for a few minutes before popping each puto out of its mold. By that time, the whole family is already gathered in the kitchen, ready and waiting.

questions.questionText: What does Nanay do FIRST when making puto?

choices.choiceText: She greases the molds with butter
She measures rice flour and mixes it with sugar and salt
She puts the molds in the steamer

questions.questionText: What happens AFTER Nanay adds baking powder?
Why does Nanay only fill each mold three-quarters full?

choices.choiceText: The puto will rise while steaming
The cheese needs space on top

questions.questionText: What is the LAST thing Nanay does before the family eats the puto?

choices.choiceText: She steams the puto for 15–20 minutes
She lets the puto cool and pops them out of the molds

questions.questionText: Which signal word tells you what Nanay does at the beginning?
Sequencing means arranging events in ________.
Nanay mixes rice flour with sugar and a pinch of ________.
Nanay greases the molds with a little ________ so the puto slides out easily.
The puto is cooked by ________ for fifteen to twenty minutes.
Words like 'first,' 'next,' 'then,' and 'finally' are called ________ words.
Nanay adds ________ milk to the batter while stirring.
The puto is done when the tops ________ back lightly when touched.

lessons.title: Same and Opposite
lessons.passageText:
Words are like tools. Just as a carpenter chooses the right tool for each job, a good writer chooses the right word for each sentence. Two types of words that help writers do this are synonyms and antonyms.

Synonyms are words that have the same or nearly the same meaning. For example, 'happy' and 'joyful' are synonyms. So are 'big' and 'large,' 'begin' and 'start,' and 'fast' and 'quick.' Using synonyms makes writing more interesting because it helps a writer avoid repeating the same word over and over.

Antonyms are words that have opposite meanings. 'Hot' and 'cold' are antonyms. So are 'loud' and 'quiet,' 'day' and 'night,' and 'brave' and 'cowardly.' Understanding antonyms helps readers understand contrast — the difference between two very different ideas or things.

Here is a short story that uses both: Carlos was a timid boy who was afraid of dogs. One afternoon, he met a gentle puppy named Kiko. Slowly, Carlos grew bold. By the end of the week, the formerly fearful boy was now brave and confident.

In that story, 'timid' and 'bold' are antonyms. 'Fearful' and 'timid' are synonyms. Noticing these kinds of word relationships helps readers better understand what they read.

questions.questionText: Which pair of words are SYNONYMS?
Which pair of words are ANTONYMS?
In the story about Carlos, 'timid' and 'bold' are ________.
Why do writers use synonyms?

choices.choiceText: To avoid repeating the same word

questions.questionText: In the story, 'fearful' and 'timid' are ________.
Which is a synonym for 'quick'?
Words that have the same or nearly the same meaning are called ________.
Words that have opposite meanings are called ________.
The antonym of 'brave' is ________.
A synonym for 'happy' is ________.
Carlos grew ________ after spending time with the gentle puppy Kiko.
Understanding antonyms helps readers understand ________ between two different ideas.

lessons.title: Putting the Clues Together
lessons.passageText:
The school library was quiet except for the soft sound of pages turning. Nena sat at a corner table surrounded by books about volcanoes. She had three pages of notes already filled and was still taking more. Her pencil moved quickly across the paper.

On the cover of one book was a photograph of a volcano erupting at night, its bright orange lava lighting up the sky. Nena had circled several paragraphs and written small notes in the margins. She had borrowed four books from the shelf, and two of them were now marked with small sticky notes.

Her teacher had given the class a research project two weeks ago. Most of her classmates had barely started. Nena, however, was almost done.

When the bell rang, Nena carefully placed her notes inside a folder labeled 'Volcano Project.' She returned the books to the librarian's desk and thanked her.

"See you tomorrow," Nena said.

The librarian smiled. "I thought you might say that."

Nena walked out with her folder tucked under her arm, already thinking about what she needed to add.

questions.questionText: What conclusion can you draw about Nena?

choices.choiceText: She dislikes going to the library
She is hardworking and dedicated to her studies
She is writing a story about lava

questions.questionText: What does the librarian's comment 'I thought you might say that' tell you?

choices.choiceText: The librarian is surprised to see Nena
Nena visits the library often and is well known there
The librarian does not want Nena to return

questions.questionText: Which clue best supports the conclusion that Nena is ahead of her classmates?

choices.choiceText: She had already filled three pages of notes while most classmates had barely started

questions.questionText: When drawing a conclusion, a reader uses ________.

choices.choiceText: clues from the text and their own thinking
only what the author directly states

questions.questionText: What is the topic of Nena's research project?
Drawing a conclusion is different from summarizing because it requires ________.

choices.choiceText: copying the text word for word
going beyond what is stated and figuring something out

questions.questionText: Nena sat at a corner table surrounded by books about ________.
Nena placed her notes inside a folder labeled '________ Project.'
Using clues from the text to figure out something not directly stated is called ________.
Nena had circled several paragraphs and written small notes in the ________.
The cover of one book showed a photograph of a volcano ________ at night.
Nena walked out with her ________ tucked under her arm.

lessons.title: What Pictures Tell Us
lessons.passageText:
When we read, we do not only look at words. We also look at pictures, diagrams, charts, and other visual elements. These visuals are not just decorations — they give us important information that helps us better understand what we are reading.

A line drawing in a science book might show the parts of a flower. A map in a social studies book helps us understand where places are. A timeline shows us when events happened in history. A bar graph in a math book shows us how numbers compare.

Good readers pay close attention to visual elements. They read the labels and captions. They look at colors and shapes to understand what they mean. They ask: What is this image showing me? How does it connect to the text?

Visual elements also include things like bold or italic text, headings, and numbered lists. These help organize information and guide the reader's eye to what is important.

For example, imagine a passage about planting a garden. Next to the text is a diagram of a seed being planted in soil. Arrows show the direction of root growth downward and stem growth upward. Even without reading the full text, the diagram tells you a great deal about how a seed grows.

Learning to read visual elements is a skill that helps you in all subjects — not just reading.

questions.questionText: What is the main purpose of visual elements in a text?

choices.choiceText: To make the book look colorful
To give important information and help readers understand
To replace all the words in the text

questions.questionText: Which visual element would best show when events happened in history?
In the garden diagram, what do arrows pointing downward show?
Which of the following is an example of a visual element?
What question should a good reader ask when looking at a visual?

choices.choiceText: How many pages does the book have?
What is this image showing and how does it connect to the text?

questions.questionText: Bold or italic text, headings, and numbered lists are examples of visual elements that ________.

choices.choiceText: help organize information and guide the reader
are only used in math books

questions.questionText: A ________ in a social studies book helps us understand where places are.
A ________ graph shows how numbers compare to each other.
Visuals are not just decorations — they give us ________ information.
Good readers read the ________ and captions found near images.
A line drawing in a science book might show the parts of a ________.
Learning to read visual elements is a skill that helps you in all ________.

pre_post_questions.questionText: Which word is a SYNONYM for 'large'?
An action word is called a ________.

choices.choiceText: Putting events in the order they happened

pre_post_questions.questionText: Which word is an ANTONYM for 'brave'?
A word that describes a noun is called an ________.
Why are visual elements like graphs and diagrams important?

choices.choiceText: They make the page look pretty
They give important information to help you understand
They tell you when to stop reading
